replies.author: @Anthonytheredneckscientist
replies.text:
Robert sadly isn’t with us anymore, these coil jet stoves they are slightly finicky. First off your copper pipe sounds to large. You coil should only need to be 1/4in od. ( the bigger you piping diameter and length the more heat you need before it starts to work. 
If you build the coil too large, it won’t build up enough heat to vaporize the fuel of choice. Next the little hole you drill for the fuel vapor to jet out of, that orifice size is also quite important, the how to videos don’t really explain that, but if you orifice I’d too large the burner won’t build up enough pressure inside the coil to do anything really kinda like what’s going on here however he may have intentionally done that to make it “kinda safer” the work principle behind these stoves is very similar to the Svea 123 or the Walker G53S. You don’t want your wick to be packed to tightly within your coil it needs to be snug but not packed tight

author: @osmbsmy.706
text:
Wouldn't it be better if the outer pipe were heat insulative to make sure only the small quantity of IPA is exposed to heat to encourage evaporation?  
There's a danger of vapor forming in the outer chamber from heating the outer pipe.  This will either push vapors up the sides through gaps or worse, if the chamber is sealed, it may push liquid IPA up the center of the tube into the burning section which can get out of hand.

This should probably be explained so someone doesn't hurt themselves.

author: @RobertDinTulsa
text:
Simple reminder: 
The plant pots cannot create heat. 
They can only temporarily store heat before it radiates it.
Plant pots add ZERO value to a DIY heater.

author: @Mike80528
text:
Be careful to enclosing any of those alcohol heaters in ceramic pots. as the alcohol heats it will pressurize the container, accelerating the burn creating more heat...and can become a runaway action akin to a jet, Ask me how I know...

Also, I suspect the loop will work if you cut off the extra copper feeding into the jar and just let the wick drop down. The volume of fuel isn't the issue, it's the heat sink from the amount of copper pipe in the alcohol. It's keeping the loop from getting hot enough.

author: @aCalmHinduCow
text:
thinking out loud here, and correct me if I'm wrong, but it is my understanding that methanol has a higher energy density, while ethanol has a higher energy content. ethanol burns cleaner with less carbon monoxide and will burn longer, possibly up to twice as long as methanol. I believe it is a better fuel for these types of alcohol burner applications. methanol burns hotter, but for something like this that's using a terracotta pot that will hold the heat and emanate it over a longer period of time, I think ethanol makes sense. methanol may put out more heat, and I believe it costs less than ethanol, however, if you need a burner that will last throughout the night, ethanol may be a better choice as the methanol may burn out much sooner, though a larger reservoir may make up for that. still, I'd go with the fuel that produces less carbon monoxide if it's being used indoors.

can someone fact check me on all this? most of these answers I got from AI which is sometimes less than accurate.

author: @billunderwood5453
text:
Here's what I've learned about heating from the internet, which of course would never lie to me!
12 pounds of Anthracite Coal has about 144000 BTUs, equal heat content to 1 gallon of fuel oil.
Methanol, about 70,000 btus per gallon. 
Hydrogen, about 103,000btus per gallon (equivalent)
Propane, 91,500 btus per gallon.
Kerosene, 131,000 per gallon
Natural gas, 136,000 per gallon (equivalent).
Electrical resistance heaters produces 3413 btus per kwh, about 57 btus/minute. 40 kwh produces the same btus as 1 gallon of heating oil, about 140,000.
HHO has not been as well studied, (and written off as quack science because of people hoping to run their cars on water.) The best figure I can come up with is about 70 btus per liter.  According to pages describing the ‘best’ HHO generators, it takes about 180 watts to produce 1 liter per minute of HHO. 
I’m terrible at math; but if I’m getting this right, you could use an HHO generator to produce gas to heat a room at 70 btus/minute for 180 watts instead of an electric heater producing 57 btus/minute at a cost of 1000 watts. 
Can that be right?

author: @cdorcey1735
text: I was skeptical about the value of putting a pot over a flame, until I realized that the heat from a flame rises quickly to the ceiling without warming much of anything else along the way.  If the pot intercepts the rising hot gas, it gets warm and radiates where you want the heat.  As far as the closed room is concerned, it's the same heat of combustion, but the pot keeps more of the heat where you can feel it.  But, don't let the cat knock it over!

author: @dennisdanich7190
text:
I have a French LeGrillon Alcohol stove, it came with a 25mm cotton wick, I replaced it with a wick made of wood stove door gasket material.
I make coffee every morning using it to heat my Moka pot , I can fry eggs
Burgers, cook a pork loin in a half hour. This stove puts out heat. A small tent is warmed with one burner, adjust the flame to burn blue an little
CO2 is created. Smaller heaters can be made with 1/2 inch wicks
for personal warmers. Alcohol is a safe fuel, the French stoves were packed with horse hair to prevent a pool of fire forming if tipped, I use fiberglass insulation. Alcohol fire is quickly extinguished with water and will not burn on water like some fuels will do. I intend on building a stove like you designed. THANKS!

You MUST include a vent hole or two and keep your reservoir protected from heat. Be careful! Best wishes from California.

author: @derghiarrinde
text: what if you fed oxygenated air (=oxygen) through that central tube that would be bent 90deg on top down to where the flame starts? Or bring another oxygen tube down that flame and release heated oxygen near the base of the flame... You would have to shut off other oxygen entries to make sure the only oxygen it gets is a hot one. That way you not only preheat the fuel but you also preheat the oxygen. You would essentially be able to burn a heavier fuel even more officiently.

author: @1JasonBradly
text:
The very first thing I did when I saw the forever wick was to make wicks for my Methyl hydrate  coil burner. I had an issue where I used a 5” tall jar and it would always go out and not burn the bottom 1/3 of the fuel. The forever wick did no better in drawing the fuel up to the copper to be vaporized and burnt. In your jar experiment I believe your wick was not pushed into the coil far enough. For I have made a coil burner in a 2L jar successfully though the longest “oil lamp” wick I could get was 5” long. There again it seemed when the space between the fuel level and the top was 3” or better the burner stopped. That when I realized it can only wick so high. This idea is intriguing, thanks. 
Edit: Also in my coil burners when I had the copper tubes ends submerged then too it would not operate.

author: @WarrenG007

hey there Robert what your experimenting with is basically what I have in as a heating system in my house ,it's called "a brick central" the fuel I use is heating oil it's gravity fed and the reservoir is fed in from the base and the control is is done via a standard oil control valve that are used on a aga it's made by a company called HESS  .
the reservoir is a bit like a cooking pot a number of holes are around the base it also uses a balancing air valve all of this is done without any electric it allso has a coiled water pipe that heats my water by using the thetmo siphon principal.

author: @bohabdestructo7489
text: There is a danger of the alcohol vaper pressure over coming the static pressure of the atmosphere.  In other words if could boil over. I had it happen to me with a burner I made where the wick was too tight to the threaded cap. The vapers caused the wick to become super saturated.  It caused a run away effectively over heating the container by surrendering the container with fire. The hotter it got the faster the fuel came out

replies.author: @bohabdestructo7489
replies.text: Always use a vented container to equalize the pressure.

replies.author: @bohabdestructo7489
replies.text: I was extremely lucky that I had put my burmer on a cookie sheet, and I was on concrete.

replies.author: @bohabdestructo7489

author: @habsishpeeweed6562
text:
So yet another YouTube posting saying using a candle or possibly two plus a flower pot is going to make much difference to the heating of your home.

Yes they might keep the frost off your greenhouse or cold frame.  But much difference to you home?

author: @LOFIGSD
text: Some great ideas, liking the copper with holes, copper pipe not something I have to hand, ideas for other household items to substitute? I tried similar setup with a camping heater, which is the same idea as your mantle, 16 quid on Amazon.

author: @russellheisey4659
text: The Germans are gonna need a lot of those this winter. I used a metal trashcan with a rolled up paper towel as a wick with ethanol fuel during a winter where they cut the steam off from our unit just so I could heat up the bathroom as needed. Just be sure to have plenty of fresh air so as not to asphyxiate yourselves!

author: @wmsatic
text: Any thoughts regarding how a family, using this for emergency heat, safely shuts it off after a few hours? It seems like spills are a likely result each time it is touched, by family or pets, especially when hot. Containing flaming spills? Does Methanol tend to self extinguish when spilled or does it continue to flame? Basically it seems like this series can use a "What to plan for when things go wrong or to reduce the likelihood/severity of things going wrong?"

replies.author: @LOFIGSD
replies.text: A fire blanket and extinguisher would be prudent to have to hand and CO meter, these things fairly get going, Chinese Diesel heaters probably a safer option for using a flamable liquid indoors, or a portable gas fire.

replies.author: @lagunafishing
replies.text: You could always place it against a wall and chain it to prevent it getting knocked over.

author: @A5kY0urs3lf1st
text: Hi Robert. Everyone needs a grandad like you! Your just such a fun quirky stress free guy! Great family father/son/daughter fun projects to build and learn at the same time. My question is Robert. What gauge is stainless steel. Does it matter? Ooo and how long would that glass you had of alcohol last burning?

replies.author: @stevenc5227
replies.text: @Bill the grandad that keeps blowing up the shed you mean lol. I also wondered how long that heater would burn for Ethanol Methanol is around £13 a gallon and that's the cheapest i could find, so would have to last a long time to be worth it. Also that heater would be ok for outside garage or workshop, but not in a house the ceiling would be turning black after a while, its not 100% clean.

author: @RupertBruce
text: Another for the steam punk collection 🙂 You could use a brass end-cap as a snuffer and reduce evaporation when not in use.

replies.author: @aCalmHinduCow
replies.text:
Yeah, I'm not clear on how one would safely snuff this out if they needed to. Slide a larger diameter pipe that has a cap on the top of it over top of the pipe with the 1 mm holes? 

Also, those living in a small space would need something that can be kept safely out of the way and out of traffic and not accidentally be tripped over or bumped in some way.

 It would also be really cool if a Big Buddy heater could be safely converted to work on fuels other than propane, which is getting expensive. I wonder if they can be powered by ethanol somehow.

author: @agoogleaccount2861
text: Been toying around with heat exchangers etc.  . ethanol has a desire to permeate terra Cotta efficiently and naturally. I used to used crushed flower pots or an aquarium gravel made of porous material  in an iron pot soaked with alcohol for plastic bending   Ever thought about a wick from the carbon put in a tiny terra Cotta pot or on a bathroom tile instead  it'll vaporize Fuel  very well

author: @daniellow426

replies.author: @dismayedtrinket2518
replies.text:
Depends what he is burning. The energy output of various fuels is easy enough to look up online.

If it is a blue flame it is operating at 100% efficiency. If it is yellow it will be slightly less.

Depending how you engineer your burner is going to dictate how quickly the fuel is burned and the energy released.

author: @SolarSeeker45
text: If you think that's good waste oil burners are insane! Just take a length of flue pipe and set it over a little tin of old motor oil then add a half shot glass of kerosene to help it ignite. This is definitely an outdoor project but it gets wicked hot.

author: @sk8pkl
text: What fuel burns and dosent give off carbon monoxide?

replies.author: @jimurrata6785
replies.text:
Any fuel that is completely combusted.
CO can only be created if the mix is too rich, because carbon monoxide is a fuel in itself.
